explanation_md: Without braces, only the first statement after `if` is conditional. `A` prints because `x > 0` is true. `B` prints unconditionally because it is a separate statement outside the `if` body — indentation does not affect scope in C++.
`x` is -2, so `x > 0` is false. The `if` body (only the `A` line) is skipped. `B` is not inside the `if` — no braces — so it always executes regardless of the condition.

options.b: The last `cout` is not inside the `else` block — it always runs due to missing braces
options.c: You cannot use `<<` twice on the same line
options.d: The `else` branch is missing a semicolon

explanation_md: Without braces around the `else` body, only the first statement belongs to `else`. The `x = ...` line is a separate statement that always runs. Adding `{ }` around both branch bodies fixes it.

prompt_md: In a chain of `if / else if / else`, how many branches execute when the first condition is true?

options.a: All branches whose conditions are true
options.b: Only the first branch whose condition is true
options.c: The first branch and the final `else` branch
options.d: All branches in order, skipping only false ones

explanation_md: In an `if / else if / else` chain the conditions are tested top to bottom and execution enters the first true branch, then skips the rest. At most one branch runs.
